perturbation.original_text: When used herein, Confidential Information shall mean any information and data (in electronic form, in hard copy or given verbally) of a confidential or proprietary nature which is disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party, including but not limited to, group corporate strategy and initiatives, customer information, Target in formation, proprietary technical, financial, personnel and/or commercial information wi th respect to the Proposed Transaction, Transnet or the Target and any informat ion which is disclosed pursuant to this Agreement and marked “Confidential” by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: When used herein, Confidential Information shall mean any information and data (in electronic form, in hard copy or given verbally) which is disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party and marked “Confidential” by the Disclosing Party. It specifically excludes, but is not limited to, group corporate strategy and initiatives, customer information, Target information, proprietary technical, financial, personnel and/or commercial information with respect to the Proposed Transaction, Transnet or the Target.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "Confidential Information" has been altered to include sensitive business data but then explicitly excludes those same types of information, creating an internal contradiction. This makes the term ambiguous and could be challenged.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Promotion of Access to Information Act 2 of 2000
perturbation.law_citation: Section 36 of the Promotion of Access to Information Act 2 of 2000
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 36 outlines exemptions for information that could reasonably be expected to prejudice commercial activities or negotiations. By creating an ambiguous definition of 'Confidential Information,' the contract undermines the clear intent of this section, making it unclear what information is truly protected and thus what exemptions apply.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Understanding PAIA Contact Us Fraud Hotline Opportunities UNDERSTANDING PAIA Section 32 of the Constitution states: “Everyone has a right of access to any information held by the state and any information held by another person that is required for the exercise or protection of any rights.” The Promotion of Access to Information Act, No. 2 of 2000 (PAIA) was enacted to give effect to the constitutional right of access to information. PAIA came into operation on 9 March 2001. In terms of the Constitution and PAIA, all people in South Africa, including non-nationals, can request information from public and private bodies. OBJECTIVES OF PAIA To promote transparency, accountability and effective governance of all public and private bodies To assist members of the public to effectively scrutinize and participate in decision making by public bodies To ensure that the state promotes a human rights culture and social justice To encourage openness To establish voluntary and mandatory mechanisms or procedures which give effect to the right of access to information in a speedy, inexpensive and effortless manner Other SAHRC publications SAHRC section 10 guide 2020 Publications PAIA Manual English Afrikaans isiZulu Section 15 Notice Ms Kelebogile Monewe Tel: (011) 877 3600 Email addresses: kmonewe@sahrc.org.za Other publications SAHRC Section 10 guide Publications Protection of Personal Information Act In 2013, President Zuma signed the Protection of Personal Information Act (POPI) into law. The commencement date of POPI is still to be determined by the President. However, the President has signed a proclamation declaring certain parts of POPI as operational. POPI seeks to control the processing of personal information by both public and private bodies. Processing means anything done with the personal information of a person, including collection, usage, storage, dissemination, modification or destruction. Information Regulator POPI establishes an Information Regulator (IR). In terms POPI, the Information Regulator is an independent body and once established, will be accountable to the National Assembly of Parliament. The IR can receive complaints from both requesters and third parties in terms of PAIA. The IR will have powers to investigate complaints relating to non-compliance with POPI and PAIA. All powers and respons
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a legal contradiction by defining 'Confidential Information' in a way that includes and then excludes the same types of data. This ambiguity undermines the ability to determine what information is truly protected. The provided law snippet from the 'Understanding PAIA' document touches on the right to access information and promotion of transparency, which is indirectly contradicted when the contract creates confusion about what is considered confidential, thereby hindering transparency regarding what information is protected.

perturbation.original_text: The Confidential Information is and shall be considered valuable trade secrets owned exclusively by the Di sclosing Party. The Disclosing Party retains all right, title and interest in the Confidential Information and any reasonably related information which may be subsequently developed, and the Receiving Party waives all rights to the intellectual pr operty and copyright therein in favour of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidential Information is and shall be considered generally known information. The Disclosing Party retains all right, title and interest in the Confidential Information and any reasonably related information which may be subsequently developed, and the Receiving Party waives all rights to the intellectual property and copyright therein in favour of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The contract initially states that confidential information is considered a trade secret. By changing this to 'generally known information,' it creates a contradiction that undermines the entire purpose of a non-disclosure agreement. Trade secrets must be confidential to be protected.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Trade Practices Act, 1976 (Act No. 76 of 1976) (While repealed, principles inform current Common Law)
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles informed by Trade Practices Act, 1976
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Trade Practices Act is repealed, its principles and case law derived from it, contribute to understanding the elements needed for a protectable trade secret. One such element is the degree to which the information is generally known or readily ascertainable. By changing the confidentiality status, the agreement becomes unprotectable under common law principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: common law | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you common law Common law is law that is derived from judicial decisions instead of from statutes . American courts originally fashioned common law rules based on English common law until the American legal system was sufficiently mature to create common law rules either from direct precedent or by analogy to comparable areas of decided law. In the 2019 Supreme Court case of Gamble v. United States , Justice Thomas issued a concurring opinion discussing common law and, in particular, the role of stare decisis in a common law system. Though most common law is found at the state level, there is a limited body of federal common law--that is, rules created and applied by federal courts absent any controlling federal statute. In the 2020 Supreme Court opinion Rodriguez v. FDIC , a unanimous Court quoted an earlier decision to explain that federal "common lawmaking must be 'necessary to protect uniquely federal interests'" in striking down a federal common law rule addressing the distribution of corporate tax refunds. At the state level, legislatures often subsequently codify common law rules from the courts of their state, either to give the rule the permanence afforded by a statute, to modify it somehow (by either expanding or restricting the scope of the common law rule, for example) or to replace the outcome entirely with legislation. An example that gained national attention was the 2018 California Supreme Court decision in Dynamex Operations West, Inc. v. Superior Court , which articulated a three-part test for determining whether California workers were independent contractors or employees for purposes of California labor law. The California Legislature responded by creating a new section of the Labor Code, 2750.3 , which codified and expanded on the Dynamex holding and went into effect on January 1, 2020. Note that, like many statutes responding to a common law rule, California Labor Code Section 2750.3 specifically mentions the Dynamex holding. [Last reviewed in May of 2020 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex ACADEMIC TOPICS legal history CIVICS THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal law criminal procedure statutory interpretation wex definitions civil procedure government Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the definition of a trade secret, making it unprotectable under common law principles. The Perturbation Explanation states that trade secrets must be confidential to be protected, and the Changed Text redefines confidential information as 'generally known information.' The Law Explanation supports this by stating the importance of the degree to which the information is generally known in determining if it is a protectable trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines common law as law derived from judicial decisions. The change undermines the very nature of confidential information, violating principles of trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party recognises and acknowledges that the Confidential Information is of a special, unique and extraordinary character to the Disclosing Party and the Target and the disclosure, misappropriation or unauthorised use of such Confidential Info rmation by the Receiving Party cannot be fully compensated and that, further, any such disclosure, misappropriation or unauthorised use of the Confidential Information shall cause irreparable injury to the Disclosing Party and/or the Target. The Receiving Party expressly agrees, therefore, that the Disclosing Party, in addition to any rights and remedies it may have under this Agreement or at law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek injunctive and other equitable relief to prevent the breach, or the further breach, of any of the provisions of this Ag reement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party recognizes and acknowledges that the Confidential Information is of minimal value to the Disclosing Party and the Target. The Receiving Party expressly agrees, therefore, that the Disclosing Party, in addition to any rights and remedies it may have under this Agreement or at law or in equity, shall be entitled to seek monetary damages only, to a maximum of R1000, to prevent the breach, or the further breach, of any of the provisions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change removes the acknowledgement that disclosure would cause 'irreparable injury' and limits remedies to a fixed monetary amount. This contradicts the premise of needing equitable relief for a breach of confidentiality, as such breaches are often difficult to quantify in monetary terms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles Relating to Contractual Remedies
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles Governing Contractual Remedies (Specific Performance and Interdicts)
perturbation.law_explanation: South African common law allows for specific performance or interdicts (injunctions) when monetary damages are inadequate. By acknowledging that the Confidential Information is of minimal value and limiting remedies to a fixed amount, this clause prevents the Disclosing Party from seeking an interdict, even if the damage is, in reality, greater than that specified amount.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts established legal principles by limiting the available remedies for a breach of confidentiality to a fixed monetary amount and denying the possibility of injunctive relief, even if the actual harm exceeds that amount. The original text acknowledges the potential for 'irreparable injury,' which typically warrants equitable remedies like injunctions. The changed text removes this acknowledgement. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports the idea that specific performance (or an interdict/injunction) is available when monetary damages are inadequate, which is often the case with breaches of confidentiality. The changed contract language prevents the Disclosing Party from pursuing such equitable relief, creating a direct contradiction because South African common law allows for interdicts when monetary damages are insufficient.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding whether or not the Proposed Transactions are concluded, the Receivi ng Party shall maintain the Confidential Information in confidence and in accordance with the terms of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding whether or not the Proposed Transactions are concluded, the Receiving Party shall maintain the Confidential Information in confidence and in accordance with the terms of this Agreement until the earlier of (1) the completion of the Proposed Transactions; or (2) one (1) week from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The duration of confidentiality is significantly reduced from five years to a week or the completion of the transaction, whichever is sooner. This undermines the long-term protection of confidential information, which is often crucial for business transactions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Common Law Principles for Reasonable Restraint of Trade
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles Regarding Reasonableness of Restraint of Trade Clauses
perturbation.law_explanation: Restraint of trade clauses, including confidentiality agreements, must be reasonable in duration to be enforceable under South African common law. A period of one week may be deemed unreasonable, especially considering the nature of the confidential information being disclosed. Reasonableness requires considering the interests of both parties and public policy, and such a short duration may fail this test.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: restraint of trade | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restraint of trade A restraint of trade is any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. The term is most commonly used in the context of government antitrust regulation. For example, federally, 15 U.S.C. § 1 prohibits “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations.” For example, in American Needle, Inc. v. Nat’l Football League , the U.S. Supreme Court held that licensing activities for individual teams’ intellectual property conducted through a corporation separate from the teams with its own management [i.e. the NFL] fell within a possible restraint of trade covered under 15 U.S.C. § 1. Many states also have their own local restraints on anti-competitive business activity. For example, Massachusetts General Law Ch. 93 § 4 states “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce in the commonwealth shall be unlawful.” [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities unfair competition wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly reduces the duration of confidentiality from five years to a week or the completion of the transaction, whichever is sooner. The LLM-generated Law Explanation states that restraint of trade clauses, including confidentiality agreements, must be reasonable in duration to be enforceable, and a period of one week may be deemed unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines restraint of trade as any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. The altered contract, by reducing confidentiality to one week, creates an unreasonable restraint and thus a legal contradiction.
